solution:
The radius of a nucleus, R is related to its atomic number A as:

R=R0A13

where R0 is the nuclear radius of hydrogen.

Thus, the density of a nucleus is given by:

d=μA43πR0A133=3μ4πR03=Constant 

(Here, μ is atomic mass unit)

Hence, the ratio of nuclear density of oxygen to helium nucleus is 1:1

subject: Physics
solution:
$\mathrm{t}_{\frac{1}{2}}=20$ minutes
$\mathrm{N}=\mathrm{N}_0 \mathrm{e}^{-\lambda \mathrm{t}_2} \quad \lambda \mathrm{t}_1=\ln 3$
$\frac{2}{3} \mathrm{~N}_0=\mathrm{N}_0 \mathrm{e}^{-\lambda \mathrm{t}_2} \mathrm{t}_1=\frac{1}{\lambda} \ln 3$
$\frac{2}{3} \mathrm{~N}_0=\mathrm{N}_0 \mathrm{e}^{-\lambda t_2}$
$\mathrm{t}_2=\frac{1}{\lambda} \ln \frac{3}{2}$
$\mathrm{t}_2-\mathrm{t}_1=\frac{1}{\lambda}\left[\ln \frac{3}{2}-\ln 3\right]$
$=\frac{1}{\lambda} \ln \left[\frac{1}{2}\right]=\frac{0.693}{\lambda}$
$=20 \min$

subject: Physics
solution:
In an $\alpha$-decay, the kinetic energy of the emitted $\alpha$-particle is given by the conservation of momentum and energy as:

$K_{\alpha} = \dfrac{A - 4}{A} Q$

where $A$ is the mass number of the parent nucleus.

For substance A ($A = 200$):

$K_{\alpha, A} = \dfrac{200 - 4}{200} Q = \dfrac{196}{200} Q$

For substance B ($A = 212$):

$K_{\alpha, B} = \dfrac{212 - 4}{212} Q = \dfrac{208}{212} Q$

The ratio of the energies of the $\alpha$-rays produced by A and B is:

$\dfrac{K_{\alpha, A}}{K_{\alpha, B}} = \dfrac{\dfrac{196}{200} Q}{\dfrac{208}{212} Q} = \dfrac{196}{200} \times \dfrac{212}{208}$

Simplifying the fractions:

$\dfrac{K_{\alpha, A}}{K_{\alpha, B}} = \dfrac{49}{50} \times \dfrac{53}{52} = \dfrac{2597}{2600}$

Answer: $\dfrac{2597}{2600}$

subject: Physics
solution:
The nuclear reaction for the proton bombardment of Lithium is:

$^{7}_{3}\text{Li} + ^{1}_{1}\text{H} \rightarrow 2 ^{4}_{2}\text{He}$

The mass defect ($\Delta m$) for the reaction is:

$\Delta m = m(^{7}_{3}\text{Li}) + m(^{1}_{1}\text{H}) - 2m(^{4}_{2}\text{He})$

$\Delta m = 7.0183 + 1.008 - 2(4.004)$

$\Delta m = 8.0263 - 8.008 = 0.0183 \text{ u}$

Energy released per reaction ($Q$) is:

$Q = \Delta m \times 931 \text{ MeV} = 0.0183 \times 931 \text{ MeV} = 17.0373 \text{ MeV}$

Number of moles of $^{7}_{3}\text{Li}$ in $\dfrac{7}{17.13} \text{ kg}$:

$n = \dfrac{\dfrac{7}{17.13} \times 10^3 \text{ g}}{7 \text{ g/mol}} = \dfrac{1000}{17.13} \text{ mol}$

Number of atoms of $^{7}_{3}\text{Li}$:

$N = n \times N_A = \dfrac{1000}{17.13} \times 6.0 \times 10^{23} = \dfrac{6}{17.13} \times 10^{26}$

Total energy released ($E$) is:

$E = N \times Q = \left(\dfrac{6}{17.13} \times 10^{26}\right) \times (17.0373 \times 10^6 \text{ eV})$

$E = \dfrac{102.2238}{17.13} \times 10^{32} \text{ eV} \approx 5.967 \times 10^{32} \text{ eV}$

Rounding to the nearest integer, we get $\alpha = 6$.

Answer: $6$

subject: Physics
solution:
Using the equation of radioactive decay law,

-dNdt=λ1N+λ2N  ...(1)

-dNdt=λ1+λ2N  ...(2)

Where,

-dNdt is the rate of decay

λ1, λ2 are decay constant for two different processes

N is the number of atoms.

Now applying the decay law for the combine process, we get

-dNdt=λeffN  ...(3)

Equating equation (1) and (2) we get

λeff=λ1+λ2  ...(4)

Now the half life of the radioactive nucleus is given by,

λ=ln2T  ...(5)

Where, T is the half life of the radioactive nucleus

Using equation (5) equation (4) becomes,

ln2T=ln2T1+ln2T2

1T=1T1+1T2

1T=110+1100=11100

⇒T=10011≈9 s

For radioactive material A, NA=N0e-λ1t

For radioactive material B, NB=N0e-λ2t

Thus, the ratio NBNA=e-λ2te-λ1t=e-λ2t·eλ1t

⇒e1=eλ1-λ2t

⇒λ1-λ2t=1

Time, t=1λ1-λ2=125λ-16λ=19λ.

Hence, the value of a=9.

subject: Physics
solution:
When a radioactive nucleus emits 1 α-particle, its mass number decreases by 4 and the atomic number decreases by 2. During positron emission, the mass number remains unchanged but the atomic number decreases by 1. Similarly, during electron emission, the mass number remains unchanged but the atomic number decreases by 1.

So, the value of P=92-2-2+1-1-1

⇒P=92-5

⇒P=87.
